questionText: In which of the following years was the total number of participants (athletes) the second highest from Country C? (Number of Athletes in Hundreds from 5 Countries: Country C data — 2005: M=4.5, F=3.1; 2006: M=6.9, F=3.3; 2007: M=4.8, F=2.8; 2008: M=6.6, F=4.2; 2009: M=7.9, F=6.3; 2010: M=10.8, F=6.9)

steps.explanation: Calculate total athletes from Country C each year
Rank the years: highest=2010(17.7), 2nd highest=2009(14.2). None of the given options a-d match 2009.
steps.calculation: 2009 is second highest but is not listed as option (a)-(d)

solution.shortcut: Sum M+F for each year and rank them quickly.
solution.commonMistake: Confusing 2008 (10.8) with second highest — 2009 (14.2) is actually second highest.

questionText: What was the average number of female athletes who participated from Country B over all the years together? (Country B female data in hundreds — 2005: 4.2; 2006: 6.2; 2007: 4.8; 2008: 8.4; 2009: 5.2; 2010: 7.2)

steps.explanation: Sum of female athletes from Country B over all years (in hundreds)
Average = Total / Number of years

solution.shortcut: Sum the F-column for Country B, divide by 6; convert hundreds to actual count.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to multiply hundreds by 100 before averaging, or dividing by wrong number of years.

questionText: What was the approximate percentage decrease in the number of male athletes who participated from Country C in the year 2007 as compared to the previous year? (Country C male data in hundreds — 2006: 6.9; 2007: 4.8)

steps.explanation: Identify male athletes from Country C: 2006 = 6.9 hundred, 2007 = 4.8 hundred
Calculate percentage decrease with respect to 2006

solution.shortcut: 2.1/6.9 ≈ 0.304 → approximately 30%.
solution.commonMistake: Using 2007 as the base instead of 2006.

questionText: The number of female athletes who participated from Country E in the year 2009 was approximately what percentage of the total number of athletes who participated from Country B in the year 2008? (Country E female 2009 = 9.2 hundred; Country B 2008: M=11.4, F=8.4 hundreds)

steps.explanation: Female athletes from Country E in 2009 = 9.2 hundred
Total athletes from Country B in 2008 = M + F = 11.4 + 8.4 = 19.8 hundred = 1980
Required percentage

solution.shortcut: 9.2/19.8 ≈ 0.465 → approximately 46%.
solution.commonMistake: Using only male or only female for Country B instead of total.

questionText: In which of the following countries is the difference between the number of male and female participants second highest in the year 2006? (Year 2006 data in hundreds — A: M=6.6, F=4.2; B: M=8.4, F=6.2; C: M=6.9, F=3.3; D: M=8.4, F=6.3; E: M=7.8, F=5.2)

steps.explanation: Calculate M-F difference for each country in 2006
Rank: C=3.6 (highest), E=2.6 (second highest)
steps.calculation: Second highest difference is E with 2.6

solution.shortcut: Find M-F for each row quickly by subtraction and compare.
solution.commonMistake: Picking C (highest) instead of second highest.

questionText: The ratio between the total expenditure on Taxes for all the years and the total expenditure on Fuel and Transport for all the years respectively, is approximately: (Taxes by year in lakh ₹: 2007=83, 2008=108, 2009=74, 2010=88, 2011=98; Fuel & Transport: 2007=98, 2008=112, 2009=101, 2010=133, 2011=142)

steps.explanation: Sum of Taxes expenditure over all years
Sum of Fuel & Transport expenditure over all years

solution.shortcut: 451:586 — divide both by ~45: 10:13.
solution.commonMistake: Mixing up Taxes and Fuel columns, or not summing all 5 years.

questionText: The total expenditure of the company over these items during the year 2009 is: (Year 2009 data in lakh ₹ — Salary=324, Fuel & Transport=101, Bonus=3.84, Interest on Loans=41.6, Taxes=74)

steps.explanation: Sum all expenditure items for 2009

solution.shortcut: Add all five items for 2009 directly.
solution.commonMistake: Skipping the Bonus or Interest values since they are small.

questionText: What is the average amount of interest per year which the company had to pay during this period? (Interest on Loans in lakh ₹: 2007=23.4, 2008=32.5, 2009=41.6, 2010=36.4, 2011=49.4)

steps.explanation: Sum of interest payments over all 5 years
Average interest per year

solution.shortcut: Sum the Interest column and divide by 5.
solution.commonMistake: Adding only 4 years or making an arithmetic error in the sum.

questionText: Total expenditure on all these items in 2007 was approximately what per cent of the total expenditure in 2011? (Year 2007: Salary=288, Fuel=98, Bonus=3.00, Interest=23.4, Taxes=83; Year 2011: Salary=420, Fuel=142, Bonus=3.96, Interest=49.4, Taxes=98)

steps.explanation: Total expenditure in 2007
Total expenditure in 2011
Required percentage

solution.commonMistake: Comparing 2011 to 2007 instead of 2007 to 2011.

questionText: The total amount of bonus paid by the company during the given period is approximately what per cent of the total amount of salary paid during this period? (Bonus by year: 3.00, 2.52, 3.84, 3.68, 3.96; Salary by year: 288, 342, 324, 336, 420 — all in lakh ₹)

steps.explanation: Total bonus over all years
Total salary over all years
Percentage = (17/1710) × 100

solution.shortcut: Bonus ≈ 17, Salary ≈ 1710; ratio is exactly 1:100 = 1%.
solution.commonMistake: Comparing bonus to a single year's salary instead of total.

questionText: In the year 2007, which state had the lowest percentage of candidates selected over the candidates appeared? (Year 2007 data — Delhi: App=8000, Sel=94; H.P.: App=7800, Sel=82; U.P.: App=7500, Sel=78; Punjab: App=8200, Sel=85; Haryana: App=6400, Sel=75)

steps.explanation: Calculate (Selected/Appeared) × 100 for each state in 2007
Identify the minimum percentage
steps.calculation: Punjab has 85/8200 ≈ 1.037%, which is the lowest among all states

solution.shortcut: Compare Sel/App ratios: Punjab 85/8200 gives the smallest fraction.
solution.commonMistake: Looking at absolute number of selected rather than percentage over appeared.

questionText: The percentage of candidates qualified from Punjab over those appeared from Punjab is highest in the year: (Punjab data — 2007: App=8200, Qual=680; 2008: App=6800, Qual=600; 2009: App=6500, Qual=525; 2010: App=7800, Qual=720; 2011: App=5700, Qual=485)

steps.explanation: Calculate (Qualified/Appeared) × 100 for Punjab each year

solution.shortcut: 2010: 720/7800 ≈ 9.23% is the highest ratio.
solution.commonMistake: Not computing all five ratios; 2008 looks tempting but 2010 is higher.

modelName: Year with highest percentage in column

questionText: The percentage of candidates selected from U.P. over those qualified from U.P. is highest in the year: (U.P. data — 2007: Qual=720, Sel=78; 2008: Qual=620, Sel=85; 2009: Qual=400, Sel=48; 2010: Qual=650, Sel=70; 2011: Qual=950, Sel=80)

steps.explanation: Calculate (Selected/Qualified) × 100 for U.P. each year

solution.shortcut: 2008 has 85/620 ≈ 13.7%, clearly the highest.
solution.commonMistake: Using Selected/Appeared instead of Selected/Qualified.

questionText: The number of candidates selected from Haryana during the period under review is approximately what percent of the number selected from Delhi during this period? (Haryana selected: 75+75+55+60+75=340; Delhi selected: 94+48+82+90+70=384)

steps.explanation: Total selected from Haryana over all years
Total selected from Delhi over all years
Required percentage

solution.commonMistake: Summing only some years for either state.

questionText: For which state the average number of candidates selected over the years is the maximum? (Selected data — Delhi: 94,48,82,90,70; H.P.: 82,65,70,86,75; U.P.: 78,85,48,70,80; Punjab: 85,70,65,84,60; Haryana: 75,75,55,60,75)

steps.explanation: Sum selected candidates for each state
Average = Sum/5; since denominator is same, highest sum means highest average. Delhi has highest sum.

solution.shortcut: Compare totals directly since n=5 for all; Delhi total 384 is highest.
solution.commonMistake: Calculating averages for all states unnecessarily; just compare sums.

questionText: What is the approximate percentage of total number of candidates selected to the total number of candidates qualified for all the five states together during the year 2009? (Year 2009 — Qualified: Delhi=640, H.P.=560, U.P.=400, Punjab=525, Haryana=350; Selected: Delhi=82, H.P.=70, U.P.=48, Punjab=65, Haryana=55)

steps.explanation: Total selected in 2009 across all 5 states
Total qualified in 2009 across all 5 states
Required percentage

solution.commonMistake: Using appeared instead of qualified as the denominator.

questionText: What was the aggregate of marks obtained by Sajal in all the six subjects? (Sajal's percentages: Maths=90%, Chemistry=60%, Physics=70%, Geography=70%, History=90%, Computer Science=70%; Max marks: Maths=150, Chemistry=130, Physics=120, Geography=100, History=60, Computer Science=40)

steps.explanation: Calculate marks in each subject: percentage × max marks / 100
steps.calculation: Maths: 90% of 150=135; Chemistry: 60% of 130=78; Physics: 70% of 120=84; Geography: 70% of 100=70; History: 90% of 60=54; CS: 70% of 40=28

steps.explanation: Sum all marks

solution.shortcut: Convert each % to actual marks and sum up.
solution.commonMistake: Using wrong max marks for a subject.

questionText: What is the overall percentage of Tarun? (Tarun's percentages: Maths=65%, Chemistry=35%, Physics=50%, Geography=77%, History=80%, Computer Science=80%; Max marks: 150, 130, 120, 100, 60, 40 respectively; Total max = 600)

steps.explanation: Calculate Tarun's marks in each subject
Total marks obtained by Tarun
Overall percentage

solution.shortcut: Total = 360/600 = 60% exactly.
solution.commonMistake: Averaging the given percentages directly instead of computing weighted total.

questionText: What are the average marks obtained by all the seven students in Physics? (Physics max = 120; Physics percentages — Ayush=90%, Aman=80%, Sajal=70%, Rohit=80%, Muskan=85%, Tanvi=65%, Tarun=50%)

steps.explanation: Compute Physics marks for each student (out of 120)
steps.calculation: Ayush: 108; Aman: 96; Sajal: 84; Rohit: 96; Muskan: 102; Tanvi: 78; Tarun: 60

steps.explanation: Average = 624/7

solution.shortcut: Sum Physics marks and divide by 7.
solution.commonMistake: Averaging percentages (520/7) instead of actual marks (624/7).

questionText: The number of students who obtained 60% and above marks in all the subjects is: (Table of % marks — Ayush: 90,50,90,60,70,80; Aman: 100,80,80,40,80,70; Sajal: 90,60,70,70,90,70; Rohit: 80,65,80,80,60,60; Muskan: 80,65,85,95,50,90; Tanvi: 70,75,65,85,40,60; Tarun: 65,35,50,77,80,80)

steps.explanation: Check each student: all 6 subject percentages must be ≥ 60%
steps.calculation: Ayush: Chemistry=50% < 60% — fails. Aman: Geography=40% < 60% — fails. Sajal: 90,60,70,70,90,70 — all ≥60% — passes. Rohit: 80,65,80,80,60,60 — all ≥60% — passes. Muskan: History=50% < 60% — fails. Tanvi: History=40% < 60% — fails. Tarun: Chemistry=35%, Physics=50% — fails.

solution.shortcut: Scan each row for any value below 60; Sajal and Rohit have none.
solution.commonMistake: Missing that Ayush has Chemistry=50% or Aman has Geography=40%.

questionText: In which subject is the overall percentage the best? (Subject-wise percentages of 7 students — Maths: 90,100,90,80,80,70,65; Chemistry: 50,80,60,65,65,75,35; Physics: 90,80,70,80,85,65,50; Geography: 60,40,70,80,95,85,77; History: 70,80,90,60,50,40,80; CS: 80,70,70,60,90,60,80; Max marks: Maths=150, Chemistry=130, Physics=120, Geography=100, History=60, CS=40)

steps.explanation: Calculate average percentage per subject across 7 students

solution.shortcut: Sum each column and compare; Maths has sum 575, clearly the highest.
solution.commonMistake: Not weighting by max marks; but since the percentages are already given, simple average suffices here.

questionText: What will be the number of females above poverty line in State S if it is known that the population of State S is 7 million? (State S: 19% below poverty line; M:F above poverty line = 4:3)

steps.explanation: Population above poverty line in State S
M:F ratio above poverty line for S = 4:3, so females = 3/7 of total above poverty line

solution.commonMistake: Using M:F below poverty line ratio instead of above poverty line ratio.

questionText: If the male population above poverty line for State R is 1.9 million, then the total population of State R is: (State R: 24% below poverty line; M:F above poverty line = 2:3)

steps.explanation: Let total population = x million. Population above poverty line = (100-24)% × x = 0.76x
M:F above poverty line = 2:3, so male above poverty line = (2/5) × 0.76x
Set equal to given value and solve for x

solution.commonMistake: Using M:F below poverty line ratio or forgetting to multiply by (1 - poverty%).

questionText: What will be the male population above poverty line for State P if the female population below poverty line for State P is 2.1 million? (State P: 35% below poverty line; M:F below poverty line = 5:6; M:F above poverty line = 6:7)

steps.explanation: M:F below poverty line = 5:6; female below = 2.1 million → male below = (5/6)×2.1 = 1.75 million
Total below poverty line = 2.1 + 1.75 = 3.85 million
35% is below poverty line → 3.85 million = 35% of total. Total = 3.85/0.35 = 11 million. Above poverty line = 65% of 11 = 7.15 million
M:F above poverty line = 6:7; male above = (6/13) × 7.15

solution.shortcut: Work backwards: female below → male below → total below → total → above → male above.
solution.commonMistake: Using M:F below poverty ratio for above poverty calculation.

questionText: If the population of males below poverty line for State Q is 2.4 million and that for State T is 6 million, then the total populations of States Q and T are in the ratio: (State Q: 25% below poverty line, M:F below = 3:5; State T: 15% below poverty line, M:F below = 5:3)

steps.explanation: For State Q: M:F below = 3:5. Male below = 2.4 million → total below = (2.4/3)×(3+5) = 0.8×8 = 6.4 million
Total population of Q: 25% below → Total Q = 6.4/0.25 = 25.6 million
For State T: M:F below = 5:3. Male below = 6 million → total below = (6/5)×(5+3) = 1.2×8 = 9.6 million
Total population of T: 15% below → Total T = 9.6/0.15 = 64 million

solution.shortcut: Total Q = (male below Q × 8/3)/0.25; Total T = (male below T × 8/5)/0.15; then simplify ratio.
solution.commonMistake: Dividing by wrong ratio parts or confusing the poverty line percentage.
